question: Define software quality and explain the two main types of quality attributes as defined by Garvin.
expectedPoints: Software quality is the conformance to explicit stated functional and performance requirements, explicit documented development standards, and implicit characteristics that are expected of all professionally developed software.
The two main types are quality of design (how well the specification is implemented) and quality of conformance (how well the implementation follows the design).
Garvin's dimensions of quality include performance, features, reliability, conformance, durability, serviceability, aesthetics, and perceived quality.

question: List and briefly describe three software quality assurance (SQA) activities.
expectedPoints: Formal technical reviews: A structured meeting to uncover errors in software artifacts like requirements, design, or code.
Testing: Executing software with test data to find defects and ensure it meets requirements.
Process adherence: Ensuring the development team follows the defined software process, standards, and procedures.

question: Compare and contrast the concepts of verification and validation (V&V) in the context of software quality. Explain how a Software Quality Assurance (SQA) plan supports these activities throughout the project lifecycle.
expectedPoints: Verification asks 'Are we building the product right?' (process-oriented, ensuring work products conform to specifications). Validation asks 'Are we building the right product?' (product-oriented, ensuring the final product meets user needs).
The SQA plan provides a roadmap for all V&V activities, defining the audits, reviews, tests, and standards to be used.
It ensures V&V is integrated throughout the lifecycle (e.g., requirements reviews for validation, code reviews for verification), not just at the end.
The plan assigns responsibilities, schedules tasks, and provides a framework for managing and tracking defects found during V&V, ensuring systematic quality management.
